worth_it_explanation: Private Marketplace (PMP) and Programmatic Guaranteed (PG) deals carry negotiated rates that sit above open-market pricing, so a stalled deal ID or a misaligned floor moves real money. This check reads deal status and pricing inside TripleLift and the publisher's ad server, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete deal or floor the publisher can correct.
intro: Deals route specific buyers to a publisher's inventory at negotiated terms, and on TripleLift those deals run as PMP or PG arrangements through the exchange. This subcategory is about the health of those deals and how their pricing sits against the open market, rather than the broader buyer relationships that produce them. These checks confirm that active deal IDs are spending as expected and that deal floors and priorities are set so a deal neither undercuts the open market nor gets undercut by it.

audit_checks.title: Deal health is not monitored for stalled or non-spending deal IDs
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A PMP or PG deal that has stopped spending earns nothing while the inventory it was meant to fill clears at open-market rates instead. Without a regular read of deal status, a stalled deal ID can sit dormant for weeks, so the publisher loses the negotiated premium and only finds out when a buyer asks why their deal underdelivered.

educate.base:
A deal on TripleLift connects a named buyer to a publisher's inventory at terms the two sides agreed in advance. A Private Marketplace (PMP) deal sets up an invitation-only auction where the named buyer can bid on the inventory, and a Programmatic Guaranteed (PG) deal commits the buyer to a fixed volume at a fixed price. Each deal carries a deal ID that travels in the bid request so the buyer's platform recognizes the inventory as theirs to bid on. When the deal is healthy, the deal ID is active, the buyer is bidding, and impressions are clearing at the negotiated rate.

Deals stall for reasons that leave no obvious mark. A buyer can pause a campaign on their side, a targeting change can stop matching the inventory, a creative can fail a review, or the deal ID can expire without anyone renewing it. When that happens the inventory does not go dark. It falls back to the open market and clears at whatever the open auction pays, which is the price the deal was negotiated to beat. The revenue gap shows up only if someone is reading deal-level spend against what the deal was expected to deliver.

Monitoring deal health means reading, per deal ID, whether it is active, whether it is receiving bids, and whether it is spending close to its expected pace. TripleLift reports this in the partner console, and a publisher running deals through a Prebid header-bidding setup can also see deal activity in their ad server where the deal targeting lands. The two views together show whether a deal is working or whether its inventory is quietly clearing on the open market instead.

A few signs point to deal health going unmonitored:
  • No regular review of deal-level spend exists, so a stalled deal ID would not be noticed until a buyer raises it
  • Active deal IDs show little or no spend against an expected pace, with no record of why
  • Expired or paused deal IDs are still listed as active with no renewal or cleanup on record
You may want to confirm that deal-level spend is read on a regular cadence rather than only when a buyer asks. One approach is to pull a deal-level report on a set schedule and flag any active deal ID that has stopped spending against its expected pace.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, premium deals are often tied to specific sections or sponsorship-adjacent placements, so a stalled deal there leaves high-value inventory clearing at open-market rates. You may want to track deal spend by the placement each deal targets, so a deal that stops spending on a premium section surfaces quickly rather than blending into a site-wide total.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, deals may target product and category pages where the audience is closer to a purchase and the negotiated rate reflects that intent. You may want to read deal spend against those commercial placements specifically, so a stalled deal on high-intent inventory is caught before that inventory clears at a lower open-market price.

educate.learn_more_note: TripleLift's detailed deal-management and reporting documentation is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the active deal IDs in TripleLift and the ad server, and check each one's recent spend against the pace it was expected to deliver.

triplelift_console.label: TripleLift partner console
steps: Open the deals or programmatic section of the TripleLift partner console and list the active deal IDs with their buyer, type (PMP or PG), and negotiated rate.
For each deal ID, note whether it is active, whether it is receiving bids, and its recent spend.
Flag any active deal ID that shows little or no spend, and any deal still listed as active past its agreed end date.
Ask the publisher what spend each deal was expected to deliver, and record where actual spend falls short.

ad_server_reporting.label: Ad server reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by the deal targeting in the ad server, for example the 'hb_deal' key-value pairs from a Prebid setup, with impressions and revenue per deal.
Compare deal-level impressions and revenue against the open-market line items on the same inventory to see whether deals are winning the inventory they target.
Note any deal whose inventory is clearing mostly on the open market, which points to a deal that is not active or not bidding.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per active deal ID, whether it is bidding and how its recent spend compares to the pace it was expected to deliver. A setup where deal spend is read on a regular cadence and active deal IDs are spending close to expectation passes. A setup where active deal IDs show little or no spend with no record of why, or where deal health is read only when a buyer asks, is a fail recorded with the affected deal IDs.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each active deal ID, whether it is bidding, and its spend against the expected pace above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} does not monitor deal health on a regular cadence, so a stalled Private Marketplace (PMP) or Programmatic Guaranteed (PG) deal ID can sit dormant while its inventory clears at open-market rates instead of the negotiated premium. A deal stops spending for reasons that leave no obvious mark, such as a paused buyer campaign or an expired deal ID, and the gap shows only if deal-level spend is read against expectation. {{voice_recommend}} pulling a deal-level report on a set schedule, flagging any active deal ID that has stopped spending against its expected pace, and reconciling it against the open-market spend on the same inventory. After the review, set a recurring cadence so a stalled deal surfaces in days rather than weeks.

audit_checks.title: Deal floors and priorities are misaligned with the open market
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A deal floor set below the open-market clearing price lets the deal win inventory the open auction would have paid more for, so the publisher trades a higher open bid for a lower deal price. A deal priority that does not outrank the open market lets open bids beat a deal that was negotiated to win, so the deal underdelivers. Either way a misaligned floor or priority moves money in the wrong direction.

educate.base:
A deal sits in the same auction as open-market demand, so two settings decide whether it helps or hurts a publisher's yield. The first is the deal floor, the minimum price the buyer agreed to pay for the inventory. The second is priority, the rule that decides whether a deal bid or an open-market bid wins when both are present. When the floor and the priority are aligned, a deal wins the inventory it was meant to win at a price that beats the open market. When they are not, the deal works against the publisher in one of two directions.

The first direction is a floor set too low. If a deal floor sits below what the open auction would have paid for the same impression, the deal can win the impression at the lower negotiated price while a higher open-market bid goes unfilled. The publisher meant the deal to be a premium, but in practice it clears below the open price and the difference is lost. The fix is to set the deal floor at or above the open-market clearing price for that inventory, so the deal only wins when its price genuinely beats the open auction.

The second direction is a priority that does not match the intent. A Programmatic Guaranteed deal is a volume commitment, so it usually needs to win ahead of the open market to deliver its guaranteed impressions. If its priority does not outrank open-market demand, open bids can beat it and the deal underdelivers, which can breach the commitment to the buyer. A Private Marketplace deal, by contrast, is meant to compete on price, so it usually belongs in the same auction as open demand rather than ahead of it. Matching priority to deal type is what keeps each deal doing the job it was negotiated for.

TripleLift carries the deal floor and the buyer terms, and in a Prebid header-bidding setup the ad server holds the line-item priority that decides whether deal bids win ahead of open-market bids. Prebid's documentation describes giving deal line items a priority above open-market bids but below direct sales, and a 'preferdeals' option that picks the highest-value deal before the highest-value non-deal bid. The audit question is whether the deal floors sit against the open-market clearing price and whether each deal's priority matches its type.

A few signs point to misaligned floors or priorities:
  • Deal floors are set without reference to the open-market clearing price for the same inventory
  • A Programmatic Guaranteed deal is underdelivering while open-market bids win the inventory it was meant to fill
  • All deals share one priority setting regardless of whether they are PMP or PG
You may want to confirm that each deal floor sits at or above the open-market clearing price and that priority matches the deal type. One approach is to read the open-market clearing price for the inventory each deal targets, then set the deal floor and the line-item priority against it.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, a sponsorship-style Programmatic Guaranteed deal on a premium section usually needs priority above the open market to deliver its committed volume, while a PMP deal on general inventory competes on price. You may want to set priority by deal type per placement, so a guaranteed deal on a premium section delivers while a PMP deal elsewhere still has to beat the open auction to win.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, a deal targeting high-intent product and category pages should carry a floor that reflects the higher open-market price those pages clear at, rather than a site-wide floor. You may want to set the deal floor against the open-market clearing price for the commercial inventory specifically, so the deal does not win high-intent impressions below what the open auction would have paid.

educate.learn_more_note: TripleLift's deal-specific floor and priority configuration is documented in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review each deal's floor against the open-market clearing price for the inventory it targets, and check that each deal's line-item priority matches its type.

triplelift_console.label: TripleLift partner console
steps: Open the deals section of the TripleLift partner console and list each deal ID with its type (PMP or PG), its floor, and the inventory it targets.
Record the negotiated floor for each deal and note whether it was set with reference to the open-market price for that inventory or as a standalone number.
Flag any Programmatic Guaranteed deal that is underdelivering against its committed volume.

ad_server_reporting.label: Ad server reporting
steps: In the ad server, review the line-item priority assigned to deal targeting against the priority of open-market line items, for example the relative priority of 'hb_deal' line items versus open-market header-bidding line items in a Prebid setup.
Build a report of the open-market average clearing price (eCPM) for the inventory each deal targets, and compare it to that deal's floor.
Note any deal whose floor sits below the open-market clearing price for the same inventory, and any deal whose priority does not match its type.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per deal, its floor against the open-market clearing price for the inventory it targets and its line-item priority against its deal type. A setup where deal floors sit at or above the open-market clearing price and priorities match deal type, with Programmatic Guaranteed deals ranking above the open market and Private Marketplace deals competing within it, passes. A setup where a deal floor sits below the open-market price, or a priority does not match the deal type, is a fail recorded with the affected deals.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each deal's floor against the open-market clearing price and its priority against its deal type above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more deals on {{site_name}} carry a floor or priority that is misaligned with the open market. A deal floor set below the open-market clearing price lets the deal win inventory a higher open bid would have paid more for, and a priority that does not match the deal type lets a Programmatic Guaranteed deal underdeliver or a Private Marketplace deal win ahead of stronger open demand. {{voice_recommend}} reading the open-market clearing price for the inventory each deal targets, setting the deal floor at or above it, and matching line-item priority to the deal type so guaranteed deals rank above the open market while private marketplace deals compete within it. After the change, recheck deal delivery and the open-market spend on the same inventory to confirm the deals win the impressions they should at the price they should.
